message.content:
Timestomping is a technique where adversaries modify file time attributes (modify, access, create, and change times) to conceal their activities and evade detection. By altering timestamps, attackers make malicious files appear as if they were created or modified at the same time as legitimate files, thus avoiding suspicion during forensic analysis. This technique is often used in conjunction with file name masquerading to further obscure the presence of malware and tools.

The potential impact of timestomping includes hindering forensic investigations, delaying detection of malicious activities, and complicating incident response efforts. Common adversary exploitation methods involve using built-in commands or custom tools to change file timestamps, as seen with groups like APT28, APT29, and Lazarus Group, among others.

Mitigation strategies include implementing robust file integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized changes to file attributes, maintaining comprehensive logs of file access and modifications, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions that can identify and alert on suspicious timestamp modifications. Regular audits and anomaly detection can also help in identifying timestomping activities.
